title: The Mystery of Big G
summary: Scientists want to measure a number called Big G. Big G tells us how strong gravity is. Gravity is the force that keeps us on Earth and makes things fall. Even though we know about Big G for a long time, it is hard to measure it exactly. Different experiments give slightly different numbers. This is confusing for scientists. A group called NIST did a new experiment to try to get a better number. They used a special tool called a torsion balance. It has metal cylinders that twist when gravity pulls on them. They did the experiment with copper and sapphire masses and got the same result. But the new number still does not match other experiments. So the mystery of Big G continues. Scientists keep trying because knowing Big G helps us understand the universe better.

keywords.explanation: A number that tells how strong gravity is
The force that pulls things toward each other
A tool that twists when gravity pulls on it

questions.question: What tool did scientists use in the new experiment?
What did NIST scientists use to make the masses?
Why is it hard to measure Big G?
Did the new experiment fix the problem with Big G?
options: Yes, now we know the exact value
No, it still doesn't match other experiments
Yes, the number is now perfect

questions.question: Who first measured Big G in a lab?

background_read: Gravity is a force that pulls objects together. The strength of gravity depends on how much mass something has and how far away it is. Big G is a constant number that helps us calculate that force. Scientists have been trying to measure Big G very accurately for over 200 years. Different experiments give slightly different results, which is a puzzle.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists at NIST and other labs
WHAT: Trying to measure Big G more precisely
WHERE: In laboratories using a torsion balance
WHY: To understand gravity better and resolve discrepancies

perspectives.description: They want a single accurate number for Big G
It's weak and hard to measure without interference
